metrics.narrative: Gary Chew articulated quantified pain on the 2026-06-08 call: approximately 50% of Keppel's tail spend comprises inventoried MRO items that fall below sourcing thresholds and remain unmanaged. Current spend analysis requires manual extraction from SAP and Ariba into Excel, a time-intensive process. Data enrichment is inaccurate with duplicates due to inconsistent email formats. Ariba's weak, non-intuitive user interface frustrates the procurement team. SAP supplier portal lacks user-friendliness, hindering onboarding and incurring additional costs. Gary explicitly stated that small-value purchases below thresholds leave low-value tail spend unmanaged, and negotiations remain offline despite platform support due to complex contract templates. These are operational pain points with clear process-cost implications (manual effort, supplier friction, unmanaged spend leakage). The business case for Intake/ANA/Sourcing to complement Ariba is grounded in eliminating manual workarounds, improving tail-spend capture, and delivering a better user experience. No formal ROI workshop has been conducted, but the quantified pain (50% tail spend, manual extraction burden) provides a solid foundation for a savings case. Next move: build the ROI model tying Intake/ANA adoption to tail-spend capture rate and manual-process reduction, and present it alongside the pricing calculation Gary requested.

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and the only named competitor. Keppel is a current Ariba customer using it for sourcing, contract management, and supplier engagement. On the 2026-06-08 call, Gary framed the evaluation as 'current Ariba users who want to look at Intake/ANA and Sourcing' — a complement strategy, not a replacement. Carl asked whether Zycus Intake can layer over Ariba's contract/supplier modules, and Tanvi confirmed iSaaS integration capability. Gary explicitly raised Ariba's weaknesses: the user interface is weak and non-intuitive, the supplier portal is not user-friendly and incurs additional costs, and Ariba does not address tail-spend management (50% of Keppel's MRO spend falls below Ariba's sourcing thresholds). These are buyer-stated pain points that Ariba has failed to solve, creating the opening for Zycus. The competitive wedge: Zycus Intake/ANA/Sourcing complements Ariba's strengths (contract, supplier master) while solving its weaknesses (tail spend, UX, autonomous sourcing for MRO). The risk: Ariba is embedded (SAP S/4 HANA integration, existing workflows), so any Zycus win requires proving coexistence without disrupting Ariba's value or triggering an SAP/Ariba contract violation. If the iSaaS integration demo cannot prove seamless layering, or if SAP/Ariba pushes back on third-party middleware, the complement story weakens and Keppel may default to the incumbent. Gary's request for a pricing calculation also signals cost sensitivity; Ariba may defend by bundling tail-spend modules at a discount or by arguing that adding Zycus increases total cost of ownership. The activation: deliver the post-2026-06-29 demo proving Intake/ANA layering over Ariba without rip-and-replace, quantify the tail-spend capture ROI Ariba cannot deliver, and price competitively against an Ariba upsell. Position Merlin Intake as the AI-native differentiation Ariba lacks (autonomous sourcing, intelligent intake) and tie it to the quantified pain Gary articulated (manual extraction, unharmonized codes, weak UX).

identify_pain.narrative: Gary Chew (Head Projects Procurement, Decision Maker) owns the pain and articulated it in detail on the 2026-06-08 call. The operational pain: Keppel's tail spend (approximately 50% of total, primarily inventoried MRO items) falls below sourcing thresholds and remains unmanaged, leaking value. Spend analysis is manual, requiring procurement to extract data from SAP and Ariba into Excel — a time-intensive, error-prone process. Data enrichment is inaccurate with supplier duplicates caused by inconsistent email formats. Ariba's user interface is weak and non-intuitive, frustrating the procurement team and slowing adoption. SAP's supplier portal is not user-friendly, hindering onboarding and incurring additional costs. Negotiations remain offline despite platform support because contract templates are too complex for Ariba's workflow. These are confirmed, buyer-stated pains with clear cost and efficiency implications: wasted procurement hours, unmanaged spend leakage, supplier friction, and a poor user experience that undermines platform ROI. The pain is tied to the procurement team's day-to-day work (manual extraction, data cleanup, offline negotiations, supplier complaints), making it visceral and urgent. Gary's commitment to accelerate post-2026-06-29 and his request for a proposal write-up, demo, and pricing signal that the pain is driving active evaluation. The inferred pain: if Keppel does not address tail-spend leakage and manual process cost, procurement will remain a bottleneck as the business scales, and the Ariba investment will continue to underdeliver. Quantifying the manual-process cost (FTE hours, error rate) and tail-spend capture opportunity in the pricing/ROI deliverable will convert this pain into a compelling business case.

decision_criteria.narrative: Gary articulated decision criteria on the 2026-06-08 call, anchored to Keppel's current Ariba deployment and its gaps. The evaluation centers on: (1) complementing Ariba's sourcing/contract modules WITHOUT replacing them (Gary explicitly asked whether Zycus Intake can layer over Ariba contract/supplier modules; Tanvi confirmed iSaaS integration capability); (2) addressing tail-spend management for the ~50% of MRO items below sourcing thresholds; (3) improving user experience (Ariba UI is weak and non-intuitive); (4) enabling autonomous sourcing despite unharmonized commodity codes across plants (Gary raised this as a technical hurdle); (5) integrating with SAP S/4 HANA via iSaaS middleware (Tanvi confirmed capability on the call). Carl noted that Keppel wants to see how Zycus Intake, ANA (autonomous negotiation agent), and Sourcing deliver value on top of the existing Ariba footprint, not as a rip-and-replace. The criteria are use-case driven (tail spend, MRO automation, supplier onboarding friction) rather than feature-checklist driven. Gary committed to a post-2026-06-29 demo and proposal review; the criteria will be validated in that session. Risk: the technical feasibility of iSaaS layering over Ariba contract/supplier modules and the data-harmonization challenge (commodity codes) have not been demonstrated live. If the demo cannot prove coexistence or if SAP/Ariba objects to third-party overlay, the fit weakens.

best_practice_check.summary: Three high-impact levers to raise win probability: (1) Convert Gary's committed group procurement intro into live engagement with the EB/sponsor before investing in detailed demos — a $450k group deal cannot close without C-level sign-off, and the group strategy review Gary mentioned increases approval complexity; map the decision-maker now. (2) Validate iSaaS integration architecture in the post-2026-06-29 demo — the complement-not-replace story is the wedge against Ariba, but it collapses if the demo cannot prove seamless layering over Ariba contract/supplier modules; prove coexistence or the buyer defaults to the incumbent. (3) Quantify tail-spend capture ROI in the pricing deliverable — Gary's pain (50% unmanaged MRO spend, manual extraction) is confirmed but not yet translated into a savings model; tie Intake/ANA adoption to FTE hours saved and spend capture rate to justify the investment vs an Ariba upsell.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the sole named competitor and the incumbent, embedded via SAP S/4 HANA integration. Keppel uses Ariba for sourcing, contract, and supplier management but is dissatisfied with its tail-spend coverage, weak UI, and supplier-portal friction (buyer-stated pain 2026-06-08). Gary framed the eval as complementing Ariba, not replacing it, creating a coexistence wedge for Zycus. Ariba's threat level is MEDIUM: it is the incumbent with deployment inertia, but its gaps (tail spend, UX, autonomous sourcing for MRO) are confirmed and driving active evaluation. Ariba has not been re-mentioned since 2026-06-08; no active Ariba defense, upsell attempt, or competitive bake-off is evident. The risk: Ariba may defend by bundling tail-spend modules at a discount or by arguing that Zycus adds cost/complexity. If iSaaS integration cannot prove seamless layering, Keppel may default to the incumbent. How we win: deliver the post-2026-06-29 demo proving Intake/ANA layering over Ariba without rip-and-replace, quantify tail-spend capture ROI Ariba cannot deliver (50% unmanaged MRO spend), position Merlin as AI-native differentiation, and price competitively. Make the complement story credible by validating iSaaS integration architecture in the demo.
